data.title: NVE Corporation: Reliable Sensor Business, Unreliable Dividend Math
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The common wisdom is that analog semiconductor companies stand to benefit from the rise in robotics demand (in factories and, more recently, humanoids and consumer robotics). ABI research estimates the robotics market growing at 14% annually, reaching $110 billion by 2030-2035, up from $50 billion today

However, in practice, the growth dynamics are more complex.

NVE Corporation's (NVEC) product portfolio gives it direct exposure to the robotics market. It provides magnetic sensors and couplers that allow robotic arms measure their tilt and position. However, competition is high, and NVE will need to find its edge in a crowded market that values established long-term relationships.

Despite its small size, the company is profitable, allowing it to self-fund operations until it finds its niche and rhythm in the expanding robotics market. The company's 10 years of consecutive dividend payments mirror management's commitment to returning capital to shareholders, but that comes with a huge asterisk, as discussed in the following sections.

Reliability

A significant portion of NVE's sales is standardized sensors and couplers, with precise specs and mature manufacturing processes. They're not at the bleeding edge of technology, but that means they're walking a safe, trodden path, with fewer chances of making strategic risks. The probability that NVE falling from grace as Intel Corporation (INTC) did is small. This is a valuable trait for buy-and-hold investors. The keyword here is reliability.

The company does offer custom-made products to certain healthcare customers, including Abbott Laboratories (ABT). The qualification and verification needed to become a supplier for medical equipment are a testament to their quality.

NVE also appears in front of the curve when it comes to the protectionist regulatory trends, further enhancing its reliability appeal. While no company is immune to changing international trade policies, the fact that NVE runs its own clean room, where it etches and deposes wafers, gives it the necessary manufacturing infrastructure base to align its production footprint with current regulatory trends. The company expects between $700,000 and $1 million in manufacturing investment tax credits this fiscal year (p.15).

Revenue and Earnings Trends

NVE's relatively consistent performance is also a sign of its reliability. The company has been profitable since at least 2005. EBITDA has mostly hovered in the high teens range for the past 15 years, with an average of $17.5 million EBITDA annually. This consistency is demonstrated by the small difference between the EBITDA mean and the $13 million EBITDA low during the pandemic period. In other words, the standard deviation is low.

Revenue is concentrated, with two customers representing 35% and 19% of sales of FY'25 (Twelve months ended March 2025). Generally, demand falls into three buckets: 1) dealers and distributors whose demand is a function of inventory management, replenishment, and depletion cycles. 2) Direct customers, whose demand is program-specific—this is where demand is influenced by design wins: the decision to incorporate NVE's devices into a customer's products, and by extension, the successful commercialization of said programs/customer products. 3) Research contracts, a revenue stream that has become less strategic recently.

In the nine months ended December 2025, NVE's revenue stood at $18.7 million, almost flat YoY (up less than 1%). A ~$145,000 increased in product sales was partially offset by a ~$71,000 decrease in research contract revenue. Both deviations seem to fall within the normal sales volatility bounds of a small-cap business.

What's Not Reliable - Dividend

Management has demonstrated a commitment to shareholder returns. Despite its small size, the company has been distributing a $1 dividend each quarter for 10 consecutive years. Dividend yield now stands at 5.9%.

Net income was down 8.1% in the first nine months of NVE's fiscal year. Gross margins were impacted by a change in the product mix. This was offset by lower spending on R&D and SG&A expenses.

Still, this profitability decline highlights something more troubling: a payout ratio above 100%. In the nine months ended December 2025, NVE paid $14.5 million in dividends, while its net income stood at $10.3 million. This translates to a payout ratio of 141%.

I don't believe that NVE will cut the dividend soon, but I also think distributions aren't sustainable. The payout ratio has been above the 100% levels for a very long time, yet the dividend remained stable.

Management has been selling assets (mainly bonds and marketable securities) to fund the dividend payments gap. If profitability doesn't improve, NVE will eventually cut its distributions. But that might be many years from now. Historically, the gap between CFO and Dividends averaged $4.5 million annually. As of December 2025, NVE held ~$41 million in Marketable securities (both long and short term). This is enough to fund the dividend payout gap for about 9 more years.

Valuation

NVE isn't cheap. It has a PE ratio of 24x. That might be average when looking at the exorbitant valuations of other semiconductor companies. However, NVE lacks the technological moat of its leading peer, and equally important, the commercial and marketing apparatus needed to land new design wins in a competitive market. NVE provides standardized, commodity-like products with a lot of competition.

As of the time of this writing, NVE has a market cap of $330 million, cash, equivalents & marketable securities of $44 million, and carries a negligible debt of about $0.7 million. This translates to an EV value of about $287 million.

Their EBITDA in the nine months ended December is roughly $14.4 million or $19.2 million annualized. This translates to an EV/EBITDA multiple of 15x, falling within peers' average.

Final Thoughts and Risks

NVE is a small-cap analog semiconductor company that sells standard and custom-made silicon to tier-1 suppliers and OEMs. Revenue is concentrated in two accounts, but historically, revenue and profitability have been consistent. This is particularly notable given the cyclical nature of the industry.

The company's US-based manufacturing footprint aligns it with the current regulatory and political emphasis on localized manufacturing. The company mentions tariff risk on its annual reports, but the influence of product mix change has been cited as the main driver of declining margins in the nine months ended December 2025.

NVE's dividend policy is particularly interesting. NVE distributes more than it earns and sells assets (namely marketable securities) to fund the gap in the payout ratio. This situation has been going on for many years, and if current capital allocation and profitability trends persist, NVE has enough marketable securities to fund the payout gap for many years to come.

Valuation isn't cheap. The dividend yield stands at 5.9%—subsidized and boosted at the expense of the company's balance sheet. P/E ratio stands at about 24x, while EV/EBITDA is roughly 15x, all indicators that the company isn't particularly cheap on a relative basis.

My rating is a "Hold", balancing the company's stable revenue and earnings track record, strong balance sheet, and emphasis on shareholder returns against unsustainable dividend, balance sheet erosion, and fair valuation.

data.title: Lemonade: Love The Tesla Collaboration But Hate Current Valuation
data.description:
A few months ago I wrote a bullish article on Lemonade (LMND) stating that I liked that this founder-led company was continuing to see revenue and customer growth with their AI-first business approach.

Since that article, Lemonade has crushed the S&P 500 as it is up over 71% while the S&P 500 is up slightly more than 2%. Certainly part of this dramatic jump in stock price is due to Lemonade’s recent collaboration with Tesla (TSLA).

Let’s dig into the details of this collaboration as I also discuss the company’s recent Q3 financials and business metrics.

Tesla Collaboration

Yesterday, Lemonade unveiled that in a collaboration with Tesla, the business will launch Lemonade Autonomous Car insurance. In this collaboration, Lemonade will offer a 50% rate cut for drivers of Tesla vehicles when the automaker’s Full Self-Driving [FSD] driver assistance software is steering. Lemonade will launch this offering in Arizona starting January 26th, and it’ll roll out in Oregon next month.

As part of this collaboration, Lemonade is getting access to Tesla’s vehicle data so they can see user vs. FSD miles. Here’s what Lemonade’s President and co-founder, Shai Wininger, had to say about this collaboration, “Teslas driven with FSD are involved in far fewer accidents. By connecting to the Tesla onboard computer, our models are able to ingest incredibly nuanced sensor data that lets us price our insurance with higher precision than ever before. Beyond the product announcement today, we’re also announcing our commitment to the Tesla community – the safer FSD software becomes, the more our prices will drop.”

Elon Musk has frequently stated he believes FSD is safer, and Tesla’s internal data supports his views (albeit with the caveat of “supervision”). The Lemonade team seems to agree with Musk; however, in their press release, they noted they support “intermittent use of FSD.” But like Tesla, Lemonade expects FSD to continue to improve over time.

I like this collaboration for multiple reasons. First, let’s talk about consumers. Broadly, I’m going to classify consumers as Tesla owners. If you’re a Tesla owner, then clearly you’ll get a good deal with Lemonade’s new insurance offering. For Tesla, I think this collaboration could validate their claims that FSD is safer. I think there are negative perspectives around self-driving and certainly around Musk himself that make consumers wary of self-driving vehicles. With access to Tesla’s data, Lemonade can validate the company’s claims and see if FSD is actually safer. I suppose you could argue, Lemonade might be biased considering this collaboration, yet I think if the data ultimately illustrated FSD was in fact not safer, then I believe Lemonade’s management team would be honest and share those findings.

Lastly, this collaboration is absolutely a win for Lemonade. It not only gives Lemonade a chance to gain new customers that own Tesla vehicles, but this collaboration gets Lemonade’s brand name out there so customers can research and get to know this industry disruptor.

Lemonade continues to disrupt the insurance industry, and this collaboration represents movement in that direction. Lemonade is moving insurers away from historical data and towards real-time telemetry. With this collaboration, Lemonade is positioning itself to be a prime insurer in the era of self-driving.

Risks

Now there are clearly risks with this collaboration. First, both Tesla and Lemonade are betting that FSD is actually safer. If that’s not the case, Lemonade will be on the hook for claims. Furthermore, Lemonade could be hurt if they begin to insure high-risk drivers that aren’t “supervising” the vehicle and thus get in an accident. Teslas have also historically been pricey to insure. Even if accidents go down, these Tesla claims could be more costly for Lemonade compared to other car companies.

A broader risk for Lemonade is that the company’s AI components fail or aren’t implemented correctly. Lemonade has always been an AI-first business, so I believe they can implement AI better than many (if not most) of the traditional insurers. However, if Lemonade’s AI and associated algorithms incorrectly assess the risks in certain geographies or with certain customer segments, then this could lead to higher loss ratios and reduced profitability.

Although, I think you could easily argue AI is the biggest reason why Lemonade will succeed. On the company’s Q3 2025 earnings call, management noted Lemonade’s loss adjustment expense [LAE], which measures the costs of handling claims as a percentage of premiums, as one example of AI efficiency. Lemonade had an LAE of 7%, which is better than the 9% of typical large carriers. With Lemonade’s AI tools, claim adjusters can handle three times the volume they could while providing customers with a quicker and more transparent experience.

That’s just one example, and clearly Lemonade is not shying away from AI. On the earnings call, here’s what co-founder and CEO Daniel Schreiber had to say about Lemonade and their use of AI, “...AI is now -- the impact of our AI deployments, I think, is now reflected on pretty much every line in our P&L. So you're quite right. We see it almost anywhere you look. We spotlighted the LAE as a way to really provide an apples-to-apples comparison, and that way you can see how dramatically different it is from the incumbency. You could look at the fact that we've OpEx -- sorry, our gross profit has gone up tenfold in the last 3 years, whereas our headcount hasn't moved, has actually moderately declined. So there are a lot of indications of something pretty dramatic happening in terms of the AI, and we see that in terms of all the efficiencies. And if you look at the life cycle within kind of the customer engagement with Lemonade, you'll see AI everywhere.”

So while some may say AI is risky or the company is overly reliant on AI, I’d say AI has been the primary reason Lemonade has been successful thus far. Now, the question is can Lemonade continue to implement AI more efficiently than their peers, which leads to my last risk.

Competition also remains a key risk for Lemonade. This collaboration may garner the attention of more traditional insurance providers and perhaps elicit those companies to ramp up their AI and FSD insurance offerings.

Financials

In Q3 2025, Lemonade generated revenue of roughly $195 million, which is an increase of 42% compared to Q3 2024. At the end of the third quarter, Lemonade had over 2.8 million customers, which is an increase of 24% compared to the prior year third quarter. Also in the current quarter, Lemonade added a record 176,000 net new customers. Lemonade’s premium per customer is $403, which is a 5% increase YoY.

Moving on, as you can see below, Lemonade’s in-force premium and gross profit have continued to improve over the last several years:

From a GAAP perspective, Lemonade is slowly getting closer to profitability as the company’s revenue growth is outpacing their increase in total expenses, which has led to a smaller operating loss in the quarter and year-to-date compared to 2024 as you can see below:

However, Lemonade continues to dilute investors as shares outstanding have increased compared to the prior year.

After this stellar quarter, Lemonade’s management updated their Q4 2025 and full-year guidance:

Lemonade’s management team continues to emphasize that the business will generate positive adjusted EBITDA in the last quarter of fiscal year 2026. The company expects IFP growth of 30% in 2026. Now, this collaboration could be just the ticket to increase the company’s IFP and perhaps shorten the timeline in which Lemonade finally achieves positive adjusted EBITDA.

Lastly, I believe Lemonade still has a healthy balance sheet as cash has risen since December 31, 2024 and the company has more assets than liabilities, as you can see below:

Valuation

Lemonade has a valuation grade of a "D-" at Seeking Alpha:

Since Lemonade remains a growing yet unprofitable business, I maintain that price-to-sales ratio is the best metric to determine valuation for this organization. As I noted in my prior article, Lemonade is nowhere near cheap, but after the company's recent stock surge in the last few months the high valuation is more difficult to justify. As you can see above, from a forward price-to-sales perspective, Lemonade is considerably higher than the sector median.

As an example, you can see Lemonade's forward price-to-sales ratio is significantly higher than competitors like Allstate (ALL) and Progressive (PGR):

One could argue Lemonade is trading at a premium given their robust growth. Many analysts are expecting continued revenue growth of about 30% in the next few years. Still, the market is now placing a Tesla premium on Lemonade, and it was already an expensive stock. I'd say it's too expensive now.

Wall Street holds a similar view, as the general consensus is a "Hold" with implied future downside, as you can see below:

Conclusion

Ultimately, I remain a Lemonade bull and believe this collaboration will be beneficial for all parties, especially Lemonade, as this gives them access to Tesla consumers and puts a brighter spotlight on the business.

I also believe this collaboration puts a bullseye on the back of Lemonade and certainly could expose the business if FSD isn’t as safe as both Lemonade and Tesla anticipate.

Lastly, after this huge run-up, I can't justify saying investors should rush in and buy this stock after this agreement. Even if investors are excited about this deal, I’d wait for the stock to pull back to in the $60 to $65 range before adding. I’m holding my shares for now and would urge investors excited about this arrangement to patiently wait for their opportunity to add.

data.title: The Market Priced In Bankruptcy; I Priced In Assets - Babcock & Wilcox And BRC
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Markets have a short memory and an even shorter attention span. When fear dominates headlines, patience rarely gets rewarded immediately, but it often gets rewarded decisively.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we hold a wide range of investments. Some represent safer, no-drama holdings that you can buy and forget, while others present a good deal of drama. If we knew beforehand which ones were the drama queens, we would avoid them, but in my decades-long investing career, I can attest to one thing—nobody bats a thousand. No matter how great an investor you are, due to reasons within and outside of your control, some investments will become problematic.

As income investors, we strongly emphasize diversification—the more sources of income you have, the less impacted your portfolio will be by the drama—if (and when) a few issues arise. This positions us well to win, despite having a few bad eggs in the bunch.

We share a lot of content on the public side of Seeking Alpha for premium subscribers to utilize towards their own due diligence, research, and investment decisions. However, there is a lot of research and analysis we keep exclusive for our Investing Group members. This mainly applies to battleground stocks because a contrarian view often gets underappreciated in the sea of negativity. Today, we celebrate the success of patience, perseverance, and emotional discipline with two such battleground picks:

1) Babcock & Wilcox (BW)

In April 2025, when Babcock & Wilcox common stock hit $0.30/share, there was widespread pessimism about the company’s future, and bankruptcy was widely speculated by the investor community. HDO had the two BW baby bonds in its Model Portfolio:

  • 8.125% Senior Notes due 2/28/2026 (BWSN)

  • 6.5% Senior Notes due 12/31/2026 (BWNB)

BWSN and BWNB experienced a heavy sell-off and traded below 50 cents on the dollar. Source

At this time, within our Investment Group, we wrote:

“BW is an asset-rich company with a number of facilities across different geographical locations serving its various business segments.

BW’s products are highly critical industrial infrastructure that are very expensive to replace; customers tend to stick to the same boilers, pulverizers, and burners, as their plants are often designed with those specifications. Maintaining and upgrading existing equipment is a significant aspect of the business, and this means the company can continue generating significant revenues for years without selling and installing new equipment. As such, it is not in the best interests for the company to consider options like bankruptcy, where liquidation agents would try to dispose of assets at pennies on the dollar. Like the case of BWRS, SPIG, and GMAB, BW can secure competitive pricing for its operating segments.” – HDO, April 6, 2025

As we expected, in two months, BW announced the sale of its Diamond Power business to ANDRITZ for $177 million, at a valuation of 1.6x sales, substantially higher than the valuation of BW at the time, and an 8x Adj. EBITDA valuation for the segment, which isn’t highly unlike any sort of distressed sale.

We wrote:

“This sale, along with other recent divestitures, underscores the hidden value of BW’s assets – value that remains underappreciated by the broader market and is not fully captured on the company's balance sheet." – HDO, June 8, 2025

BW then pursued a tender offer to redeem some of BWSN and BWNB, but the market was already happy with the steps taken by management to improve liquidity and handle maturing debt, and the bonds were back to trading with healthy market pricing.

By August 2025, the company issued a notice of redemption of BWSN, allowing patient and opportunistic investors to realize a massive gain on their investment (not to mention the fact that they got paid to wait for the market to get its emotions together), and BWNB currently trades above par.

2) BRC Group Holdings (RILY)

(formerly known as B. Riley Financial)

Many readers of this article will be familiar with the challenges faced by B. Riley Financial (recently renamed to BRC Group Holdings, Inc.) due to massive losses incurred in their investment in The Franchise Group. This led to allegations of fraud (which Bloomberg, in particular, made a very big deal out of), and short sellers were ruthless. RILY baby bonds were trading at 40-50 cents on the dollar, including one (RILYO) maturing in less than 2 months, sporting a 13.4% Yield-to-Maturity. Early in 2024, independent audits initiated by RILY cleared the company of any wrongdoing, and in December 2025, the U.S. District Court, Central District of California, dismissed the charges against RILY and Mr. Bryant Riley. The audits revealed weaknesses in internal controls, and persistent delays in completing SEC filings prompted RILY to change its auditor in Fall 2025.

Wall Street and Main Street were anticipating RILY’s bankruptcy notice at any moment. While headlines focused on investigations, short-seller noise, and reputational damage, we continued to follow the facts, the fundamentals, and the filings (these were largely missing, making it the hardest part) associated with RILY. We held numerous calls with the head of Investor Relations to closely understand the company’s plans and progress and shared regular updates within our Investing Group. We did not chase or fear sentiment; we tracked progress.

In August 2024, after one of our calls with Investor Relations, we wrote:

“the value of the company’s assets on the balance sheet is understated vs their true potential. The pursuing of strategic alternatives for the Great American Group is noted as the company’s first priority towards achieving an improved liquidity position. – HDO, August 15, 2024

Soon enough, RILY announced that funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management will be taking a 53% position in Great American Group, significantly improving the company’s liquidity profile and ability to manage upcoming debt maturities (6.375% Senior Notes Due 2/28/2025 - RILYM) and pay down its Senior Secured Debt placed with Nomura.

We wrote:

“Based on the company’s announcements, the plan to redeem all debt maturing in 2025 includes RILYM, which offers a 7.8% current yield and an (annualized YTM of 58%, and presents ~23% upside upon such redemption and presents an excellent investment for significant returns in the short term.” – HDO, September 15, 2024

RILYM was redeemed in early 2025, while other bonds maturing in 2026 and 2028 continued to trade at deep bargains. The markets continued to anticipate bankruptcy; we disagreed.

Through 2025, RILY made a series of private debt exchanges, reducing their baby bond debt outstanding by 32% while reducing their debt costs. The company also changed its auditing firm to accelerate their process to become current on their financial reporting.

On January 14th, 2026, RILY reported its third quarter, and the company filed three 10-Q forms (for Q1, Q2 & Q3 2025) within 120 days, bringing its financial reporting current and achieving NASDAQ compliance ahead of the deadline. This resulted in a massive surge in the company’s common stock price.

Q3 was a strong quarter, with Capital Markets leading the way with $65.4 million in services and fee revenues. In the first nine months of 2025, RILY reported revenues of $689.2 million (up from $567.8 million for the same period the year before) due to trading gains and lower loan write-downs. To the market’s surprise, during the first nine months, RILY reported net income of $220 million (compared to $767 million in losses during the same period last year), ending Q3 with $185.4 million in cash and cash equivalents.

RILY is now a much smaller company with the following divisions:

  • Wealth Management: This segment generated $42.4 million in revenues and $7.2 million in net income during Q3 2025.

  • BRS (B. Riley Securities): It is a middle-market investment bank that is wholly owned by RILY but independently operated. BRS has zero debt, reported Q3 net income of $41.8 million, and paid out $7.4 million in quarterly distributions, much of which was collected by RILY.

  • Great American Group: RILY maintains a 47% ownership in GAG, an advisory services firm that specializes in asset appraisal and liquidation. Funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management maintain a controlling interest in this segment, and GAG is a much more prominent firm under this structure, pursuing larger and more lucrative deals since the carve-out.

  • Targus is a tech accessories company that is the only portion of RILY’s former brands business that is still retained by the company.

  • Communications Businesses: Lingo, magicJack, United Online, and Credo Mobile are RILY’s boring businesses that operate quite insulated from all the drama associated with the parent company, generating predictable cash flows all along.

On its 10-Q, RILY reported total investments (including securities held) of $406.7 million as of September 30, 2025. RILY ended Q3 (September 30, 2025) with $1.44 billion in total debt, with $1.3 billion of it representing senior notes with staggered maturities in 2026 and 2028. During Q3, RILY reduced its Oaktree Term Loan to $62.5 million. As we have reported previously, RILY has pursued a series of debt exchanges to reduce the total amount outstanding and reduce its cost of debt through the issuance of 8% Senior Secured 2nd Lien Notes due January 2028. The 10-Q for Q3 2025 confirms the calculations, and this privately placed security carries an outstanding balance of $277 million as of September 2025.

RILY reported $54 million in interest expenses during the first nine months of 2025, a notable reduction from $70 million during the same time in 2024, mainly due to the maturity and redemption of RILYM earlier in 2025 and the repayment of the Nomura Term Loan. It is noteworthy that, as part of the troubled debt restructurings, RILY has issued warrants to purchase 913,692 shares of its common stock, indicating that the new creditors have significant skin in the game to ensure RILY’s turnaround and operating success.

RILY baby bonds no longer trade at distressed levels. RILYK matures in 2.5 months, while RILYN and RILYG mature in September and December, respectively. Not very long ago, these baby bonds were trading at 40-50 cents on the dollar amidst deep pessimism and concerns of impending bankruptcy.

  • 5.5% Senior Notes Due 3/31/2026 (RILYK) – YTM 11.6%

  • 6.5% Senior Notes Due 9/30/26 (RILYN) – YTM 15.8%

  • 5.0% Senior Notes Due 12/31/2026 (RILYG) – YTM 15.6%

  • 6.0% Senior Notes Due 1/31/2028 (RILYT) – YTM 25%

  • 5.25% Senior Notes Due 8/31/2028 (RILYZ) – YTM 24.3%

We read opinions shared by several retail investors, indicating that the baby bonds should be entirely avoided until the company turns around and delivers positive results. Yet, they all missed the positive results that the company reported all along as they worked through their problems—asset sales at top valuations, debt exchanges with warrants as incentives.

“The future is never clear, and you pay a very high price for a cheery consensus.” – Warren Buffett

Now that RILY has delivered those results on paper, RILYK is no longer a buying opportunity. RILYN and RILYG continue to offer modest upside. RILYT and RILYZ, which mature in 2028, offer +8% yields, ~24% YTM, and up to 53% upside to par, and currently trade at bargain prices. Notably, RILYZ is up 153% from the bottom.

Despite solid results, RILY still faces a steep debt wall in 2028, with the 2nd lien senior secured notes maturing in January 2028. With RILY regaining NASDAQ compliance and the recovery in its share price, the company can access the equity markets to raise capital. The company can also extend maturities by issuing new private debt backed by receivables from B. Riley Securities. With the Oaktree loan diminishing in size, the company’s Great American asset can also be used to back new debt offerings. RILY recently filed an 8-K, indicating an amendment to its Oaktree loan, featuring a carve-out where the company can repurchase its baby bonds before June 30, 2026, for up to $25 million. This may represent a small percentage of debt due but underscores the strong relationship RILY has with its creditors and the confidence they have in the company's operating fundamentals.

RILY now has more options than it did 8-12 months ago, and we remain confident that the 2028 maturities will be tackled appropriately. It is noteworthy that company insiders own more than a third of RILY common stock, with co-CEO Mr. Bryant Riley being the largest shareholder. With the new 8% second lien senior secured note issuance, including meaningful common stock warrants, creditors now have a direct economic incentive aligned with a liquid and solvent RILY.

We expect RILY to continue focusing on debt management, business segment growth, and profitability. Congratulations to all who stayed invested or continued buying despite the fear and believing in the fundamentals. We will begin reaping our rewards starting March 31, 2026. For those who stayed on the sidelines, I am sure this was an entertaining experience, but I hope it has been educational for your future pursuits.

What are we focusing on today?

As I mentioned earlier, we maintain a large, diversified portfolio of income securities. Most of our holdings are quite low-maintenance payers, with only a few that require more babysitting. In certain cases, we see a big disconnect between the market reaction and the fundamentals of the company and make our investment decisions based on the opportunity created.

One such opportunity is in the preferred securities of Compass Diversified (CODI). Early in 2025, CODI announced non-reliance on its financial reports due to internal investigations into their largest subsidiary (Lugano). Subsequently, management clarified that the investigations were focused only on Lugano, not any other CODI subsidiary. In December 2025, CODI held a conference call, announcing that the independent audits revealed that Lugano had engaged in deliberate and systemic fraud. Since the commencement of internal investigations, CODI common stock dividends were suspended, sending the common stock plunging, but preferred dividends were paid through the process.

Last week, the company regained compliance with the NASDAQ by becoming current on all its quarterly financial reports. Through the first nine months of 2025, CODI’s subsidiaries (excluding Lugano) delivered $1.4 billion in sales (up 8.6% YoY). Excluding Lugano, the company’s Adj. EBITDA was $257 million (up 5.8% YoY). The ugly part of the Q3 report was the $215 million loss, which included $155 million lost in relation to Lugano. Notably, the Lugano segment incurred $37 million in one-time costs associated with its investigation and restatement of regular reporting. CODI’s financial results aren’t good at all, but this provides a reflection of the costs associated with fraud investigation, reporting, and remediation steps. We expect the bulk of these expenses to be non-recurring, allowing the company’s other subsidiaries to deliver as expected in 2026.

CODI ended Q3 with $61.1 million in cash and cash equivalents and maintains access to $100 million (up from the reduced $60 million during its forbearance period) in capacity on its credit facility as part of its recent debt covenant amendment. Notably, the amendment allows CODI to spend up to $10 million every quarter towards preferred dividend payments, which is the exact amount for the payouts on CODI-A, CODI-B, and CODI-C.

  • 7.25% Series A, Non-Cumulative Preferred (CODI.PR.A) – Yield 10.6%

  • 7.875% Series B, Fixed-To-Float Cumulative Preferred (CODI.PR.B) – Yield 10.2%

  • 7.875% Series C, Cumulative Preferred (CODI.PR.C) – Yield 10.1%

Management is focused on debt reduction and expects to do this through organic growth across its existing subsidiaries and continues to explore asset sales. Management expects to generate $50 to $100 million of free cash flow (after interest payments, preferred dividends, and CapEx) and intends to repay indebtedness and reduce debt through 2026.

CODI is making the best of a bad situation and continues to be supported by its other robust subsidiaries that have predictable cash flows and operational stability. Recent events have resulted in the company being materially smaller than once assumed, but we expect the cash flows from existing subsidiaries to adequately support preferred dividends.

CODI-B offers a 10.2% yield and ~30% upside to par, with a floating-rate structure after April 2028, while CODI-C offers a fixed-rate coupon with a 10.1% yield and ~30% upside to par.

Conclusion

Everything doesn’t go as scripted at all times. In Die Hard (1988), John McClane had plans to spend Christmas with his family, but instead, he was forced to adapt, running barefoot over broken glass and jumping off the roof of the Nakatomi Plaza to survive an explosion.

Markets work the same way. The script changes, headlines scream, and fear takes over. What separates winners from spectators is the ability to assess facts calmly and act decisively. Our successes in Babcock & Wilcox and BRC Group Holdings were not accidents. They were the result of patience, diversification, and a willingness to step into battleground investments when sentiment was overwhelmingly negative, but fundamentals were improving.

Volatility is uncomfortable, but it is also where some of the best opportunities are born. Discipline produces big rewards, and as income investors, we get paid to wait, as we did with BW and continue to do with RILY.

Fear is loud, but fundamentals are quieter. At High Dividend Opportunities, we continue to listen to the latter and get paid hefty waiting fees. This is the power of our Income Method.

data.title: Vertical Aerospace: Facing A Binary Outlook
data.description:
Founded in 2016 in Bristol, UK, Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) is a pioneer in the electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft industry with its flagship VX4 model. Its business model involves partnering with tier-1 suppliers to manufacture and then sell its aircraft.

Since going public in 2021, EVTL has faced significant volatility and a long-term decline. Having reached an all-time high of $128 per share, the stock is currently trading merely at $5.8 per share, though it was already a sharp improvement from the $3 all-time low seen in mid-2025.

I initiate my coverage with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $5.6 suggests that EVTL is fully valued.

Financial Reviews

To begin with, I think it’s important to consider the fact that EVTL is pretty much a pre-commercial company with zero revenue as of Q3 2025. Based on my observation, this is normal overall. As in any electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) company, EVTL’s flagship aircraft, VX4, remains in flight testing mode.

As such, while EVTL remains a high-risk opportunity because of its nascent technology, investors interested in the prospect of high return in the stock may look at EVTL’s pre-order book in Q3 2025 to get a better idea of how much it could generate in future revenues. In Q3, EVTL has secured 1500 VX4 orders with roughly $6 billion in potential value under its order book, suggesting that the growth story remains intact.

As a pre-revenue OEM, EVTL remains unprofitable, with high R&D and administrative expenses playing a huge part in driving its operating loss of about £80 million as of Q3.

Liquidity is where the possible concern could be about. At the end of Q3 2025, EVTL had about $117 million (or about £89 million) in cash and equivalents, driven by a recent fundraise from the ATM (At the Market) offering. With the company expecting to spend over $235 million over the next year, or about $19.5 million a month, my calculation suggests that EVTL will have only six months of runway before needing to raise more capital, creating further dilution into 2026.

Catalyst

Overall, I believe the most important catalyst for EVTL would be the critical design review (CDR) that will happen in mid-2026, as per the management’s comment:

The first point to note is that we have already completed the preliminary design review or PDR with 75% of our components already locked in for the certification aircraft. The next step is CDR expected in mid-2026. This will lock in the final 25% of the components for the final aircraft design and the supply chain, allowing us to move towards certification and then mass manufacture. After we complete CDR, nothing on the aircraft will change.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

This milestone, in my view, essentially will be the turning point at which the company shifts from just an R&D-focused to a certification- and testing-focused eVTOL company. Passing CDR will effectively allow EVTL to de-risk its overall business, since it means that EVTL will then be ready to build the specific aircraft required for UK CAA and EASA certification.

Furthermore, with the CDR possibly in place in mid-2026 and EVTL’s further need to raise more capital, as per my earlier analysis, a strategic investment from a tier-1 partner could unlock positive share re-rating, in my opinion. This could be any of the major partners similar to the ones EVTL already works with, such as American Airlines.

Moreover, this will also validate EVTL’s capital-light model, where it shares the cost and the risk with tier-1 suppliers for design and initial developments to unlock better margins than its competitors, like Joby, which is more vertically integrated and capital-intensive, or Lilium, which faces energy-demand challenges. As such, I believe the tier-1 partnership-driven approach may give EVTL an edge in scaling up too, easing financing interests as well.

Risk

I believe EVTL is a high-risk, high-reward stock. Despite all the positive outlooks, EVTL still faces an immediate risk of running out of cash in 2026, as I mentioned earlier. With roughly $117 million of cash as of late 2025, EVTL’s runway only extends to mid-2026. With that in mind, in the absence of any major strategic investments, EVTL may potentially go through a highly dilutive financing round, driving shares lower to price in dilution and solvency risks.

Meanwhile, execution risk from the flight tests will still remain the highlight. As in any air-related incidents, any issues in flight tests could increase public scrutiny and setbacks, such as the one EVTL already experienced this in late 2023. In the worst-case scenario, a failed flight test could push the certification timeline and trigger regulatory action in the UK.

Valuation/Pricing

Given the pre-revenue stage, my target price for EVTL is driven by the following valuation-estimation assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection:

  1. Bull scenario (45% probability) assumptions - EVTL successfully passes CDR in mid-2026, securing a tier-1 strategic investment. The market further values EVTL as a leading eVTOL OEM, with its capital-light model as a key differentiation. To estimate the target price, I estimate EVTL’s implied enterprise value today by looking at its order book value, which is estimated to be $6 billion (1,500 aircraft at $4 million each). At a 30% probability of conversion into 2026, EVTL’s EV would be around $1.8 billion. With around $117 million of cash, EVTL’s equity value would be about $1.3 billion. I estimate the number of shares outstanding to increase by 30 million shares due to strategic investments to 165 million. This brings EVTL's implied target price per share to $11.
  2. Bear scenario (55% probability) assumptions - EVTL sees its flight testing hitting technical delays, contributing to its failure to secure a strategic investment, triggering a highly dilutive round. At this point, I will estimate EVTL’s enterprise value by simply looking at its liquidation value based on cash on hand and the distressed value of its IP. Considering EVTL has spent around $400 million to date, I would estimate its distressed value at 50% of that, or about $200 million. Adding the estimated remaining cash of $19.5 million at the end of the 6-month period with a $117 million beginning balance, my estimation of EVTL’s EV is around $219.5 million. The number of shares outstanding would be higher, probably by around 50 million to 185 million, to account for higher dilution from capital raises at lower stock prices, bringing the implied target price to $1.2 per share.

At a 45-55 scenario, this would bake in my overall view that EVTL is a high-risk stock. My projection yields a target price of around $5.6 per share into FY 2026, suggesting that EVTL is fully valued from the current level of $5.8. At this point, I would give the stock a neutral rating.

Conclusion

In 2026, EVTL is facing a binary outlook, in my view. The growth story will still hinge on the CDR milestone passing along with strategic capital injection at a respectable valuation to validate its $6 billion worth of order book. This will transition the stock from simply an R&D-driven to a testing-and-certification-driven story. Conversely, the bear case suggests that failing CDR could reduce EVTL down to a survival scenario where it could risk further dilution from another capital raise at a lower price per share. At this point, EVTL remains a high-risk, high-reward opportunity, and the fully valued conclusion suggests that it is best to remain on the sidelines for now.

data.title: PDO: This Nearly 11% Yielder Is Not Just For Income Investors
data.description:
Interest Rate Environment Led to a Great 2025

I last covered PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) in March 2025. I rated it a Hold at that time, as the trend of beneficial yield curve steepening seemed to be slowing, and PIMCO was reducing leverage and risk across its taxable closed-end funds. The conclusion in that article was,

I would wait for the fund to trade at a discount or for signs of yield curve steepening to resume before buying more.

The buying opportunity emerged shortly after that when the April 2025 "Liberation Day" Tariff Tantrum knocked the share price down below net asset value.

The 3-month - 10-year yield curve, a proxy for the difference between investment returns and the cost of leverage, also began steepening again, especially towards the end of 2025 as the Federal Reserve resumed cutting the Fed Funds rate.

In the steepening yield curve environment, PDO benefitted by remaining more leveraged than most of the other PIMCO taxable CEFs as the cost of leverage declined. The fund also benefitted from its relatively high exposure to mortgage-backed securities as MBS spreads narrowed from unusually wide levels in 2024. This contributed to PDO having the second-best total return of all PIMCO taxable CEFs over the past year at 14.39%, a nearly equity-like return for this predominantly fixed income fund.

Looking forward, PDO should continue to benefit from short-term rate cuts, allowing the cost of leverage to decline further. The fund remains heavily exposed to MBS, which may not experience the price appreciation of 2025 but now earn higher income after a few years of higher mortgage rates. PDO appears to be diversifying, with nearly 15% of the fund NAV invested in equities. The fund is also well-prepared to take advantage of downturns with nearly 10% in cash and cash equivalents as of year-end 2025.

PDO is not just for income investors, however. As we saw in 2025, the fund can have equity-like returns suitable for total return investors. Those who reinvest the dividends are helped by PIMCO's DRIP, which allows the monthly distributions to be reinvested at up to a 5% discount to market price. This can result in even higher total returns than shown on the charts above.

While PDO got off to a bad start at its IPO in 2021 thanks to an increasing interest rate environment, the fund has experienced a more favorable environment in the past year, earning a 5-star rating from Morningstar. With further declines in the cost of leverage, stable long-term rates, and continued low default rates in 2026, PDO can have another strong year.

Reduced Cost of Leverage

With $1.86 billion of net asset value, PDO is the third largest of the PIMCO taxable CEFs. That NAV is comprised of $2.87 billion of investments, offset by $1 billion of borrowings for a leverage ratio of 35.1%. That is the second-highest leverage of the group.

PDO borrows predominantly through the use of reverse repurchase agreements plus a small amount of credit default swaps. In a reverse repo, the bank buys securities from the borrower with the intention to sell them back at a slightly higher price later. In essence, it is a collateralized loan. At year-end 2025. PDO had 139 reverse repos on the books at an average interest rate of 4.2% and maturities ranging from January to June 2026. Reverse repo rates tend to move with short-term interest rates, which is why we see that PDO's expense ratio was 5.22% on 6/30/2025, down from 6.63% on 6/30/2024. This was an even bigger drop than the 1.0% worth of Fed Funds rate cuts in that time period. Since then, there have been 3 more Fed Funds rate cuts, or 0.75% total. The CME FedWatch Tool predicts another 1-2 cuts in 2026. Each 1% cut in the cost of leverage reduces PDO expenses by $10 million per year, or around $0.072 per share, enough to improve the coverage of each monthly distribution by about 5%.

Fund Holdings

On a duration-weighted equivalent basis, PDO is heavily concentrated in mortgage-backed securities, predominantly non-agency MBS. This is similar to two other PIMCO Funds, PDI and PAXS. Two other funds, PGP and RCS, have more conservative portfolios and invest primarily in agency MBS.

MBS spreads narrowed in 2025 for reasons I noted in March: Interest rate volatility is down (lowering the value of the prepayment option effectively given to borrowers). Also, many banks are back in the market to buy MBS, and the lack of banking crises caused fewer banks to have to sell MBS. For 2026, there is the additional catalyst of the Trump administration's request to Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to buy $200 billion worth of MBS. A "Goldilocks" environment for long-term interest rates would also be a positive for MBS values this year. Rates need to be high enough to earn decent interest income but not so high that they encourage prepayment. The 10-year Treasury note yield (US10Y) has cooperated, trading in the narrow range of 4.0-4.5% over the past year.

While PDO is still highly concentrated in MBS, they have trimmed this exposure about 11% since my March 2025 article. Replacing this was an increased exposure to foreign bonds, both developed (+2%) and emerging (+4%). PDO was the only fund in the group to significantly increase EM exposure in the past year. The emerging category contains government bonds of Turkey, Romania, Colombia, Ecuador, and Argentina, among others. There are even still bonds from Russia and Ukraine in the portfolio, marked well below par, so there is capital gain potential in the event of a peace deal. PDO (as well as PAXS, PDI, and PDX) increased investment-grade domestic bond exposure in the past year. In the case of PDO, it was up 5%. A significant new addition was the bonds of Beignet, a joint venture between Meta (META) and Blue Owl (OWL) to build a data center in Louisiana. These bonds make up 2% of the fund's NAV. I discussed this investment more in my recent article on PDX.

PDO now has about 15% of NAV invested in equity, including common and preferred stocks as well as mortgage REITs. Many of the stock positions are from bankruptcy and other reorganizations, including supply chain manager Incora and Italian bank Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMDPF). These workouts can produce capital gains for a patient holder over time. An example of this is AmSurg, which is set to be acquired by private healthcare system Ascension.

Not all the equity positions are from reorganizations. Some are from new ventures, such as a 2.9% NAV position in Mustang Express preferred shares. Mustang Express is a natural gas pipeline connecting storage hubs around Houston with LNG export facilities in Port Arthur, TX.

Finally, PDO maintains a significant cash position, with about 1.3% of NAV in Treasury Bills and 8.5% in PIMCO's internal short-term cash fund. This should provide a significant amount of buying power when opportunities arise.

Get More Total Return With The DRIP

PIMCO closed-end funds, including PDO, offer a dividend reinvestment plan that allows shareholders to reinvest their dividends at up to a 5% discount. This plan is described in detail in the fund's annual report. If the fund is trading at least 5% above NAV, the fund will reinvest dividends at a discount of 5% to the market price. If the fund is trading at between a 0% and 5% premium, dividends are reinvested at NAV, and if the fund is trading at a discount to NAV, shares are reinvested at market price.

If you hold shares through a broker, you may have to call the broker to request participation in this DRIP, as opposed to the broker's regular reinvestment feature, which would be at market price. For example, in my experience, Schwab (SCHW) requires the client to specifically request the PIMCO DRIP, while Fidelity automatically enrolls its clients in the PIMCO DRIP as long as they select dividend reinvestment in their account online.

With PDO now trading above a 5% premium, investors can get the maximum amount of extra shares by participating in the PIMCO DRIP. For example, starting with 1000 shares and assuming a constant price of $14 and NAV of $13.30, one year of reinvesting at market gets you 1115.3 shares after 1 year (11.5% total return), but one year of reinvesting at NAV gets you 1121.7 shares after one year (12.2% total return).

This is an attractive return even for investors who do not need the income. The drawback is that in a taxable account, investors will need to find some other way to pay the tax on these distributions, most of which are taxed at ordinary income rates. Based on the UNII report, somewhere between 0 and 10% could be classified as return of capital, which lowers cost basis and defers tax until selling. PDO is the third best of the group at distribution coverage over the past 6 months and fiscal year to date; however, other funds have improved their coverage in recent months.

The DRIP makes PDO an attractive choice even for investors who do not need the current income.

Risks

A market panic like the April 2025 tariff tantrum could cause the share price to drop significantly even if the NAV is not seriously impacted. An increase in short-term rates would increase the cost of leverage and reduce distribution coverage, leading to more return of capital or, in severe cases, a dividend cut. A recession would increase the risk of default probability, widening the spreads of most bonds, especially high yield and MBS, which make up a significant portion of PDO. This would reduce their mark-to-market price and impact NAV.

Conclusion

PDO had an excellent 2025, earning equity-like returns thanks to its high leverage as the cost of leverage decreased. A couple of Fed Funds rate cuts this year should continue that trend. The fund also benefitted from narrowing spreads of MBS (coming down from unusually wide levels) and high-yield bonds (staying at unusually narrow levels). The outlook for 2026 is for continued but slower narrowing of MBS spreads and low default rates for leveraged loans and high-yield bonds, which should keep spreads narrow.

In this healthy environment for fixed income, PDO could once again earn a double-digit total return with some capital gains supplementing the nearly 11% distribution yield. That earns PDO an upgrade to Buy. Investors who don't need that much current income can enhance this total return by enrolling in the PIMCO DRIP to reinvest distributions at up to a 5% discount.

data.title: When AI Stops Being 'Tech' And Starts Being The Economy - Investment Themes For 2026 Part 1
data.description:
Before I tell you what I saw in my crystal ball for 2026, keep this line in the front of your mind:

“... the big trends mostly right will not always lead to the outcome that you anticipate.”

- Mackenzie Market Outlook

And here’s the warning label I want on this entire post: adjusting your portfolio is healthy. Replacing your portfolio is usually a mistake. In other words, tweak - don’t flip the table.

The key remains the same: stay invested. Now let’s look at the first theme that matters most for 2026 - the one that’s quietly pulling the whole economy into its orbit.

The big themes that will shape 2026

A word of caution about trends

After reading about AI, nuclear, copper, and grids, you might be tempted to go all-in on the “obvious winners.” The problem is that “need” doesn’t always equal “profit.”

We’ve seen this movie before. A theme becomes real, money floods in, valuations stretch, and suddenly there are more losers than winners - especially for investors who arrive late and pay any price.

The 2026 mindset I want you to adopt is simple:

  • Stop chasing the shiny front layer
  • Start focusing on the companies that enable the trend
  • Prefer businesses that were already strong before the trend became fashionable

That’s how you benefit from the theme without becoming its exit liquidity.

AI isn’t just tech anymore - it’s macro

If 2024-2025 was the market “discovering” AI, 2026 is the market realizing something bigger:

AI is now macro

It drives capital spending, energy demand, grid strain, industrial bottlenecks, market concentration, and (yes) valuations. BlackRock’s 2026 outlook basically treats AI capex as a key driver of growth rather than a sector story.

AI buildouts require:

  • New data centers
  • Transmission lines and grid upgrades
  • Copper, aluminum, steel
  • Electricity (a lot more than people were planning for)
  • Financing (massive amounts of it)

That’s why the opportunity set is broader than “buy a tech darling and hope.” This is moving money into industrials, utilities, materials, energy, and finance - the plumbing behind the dream.

And here’s the part most people miss: this looks and feels like a capex bull market. BlackRock explicitly references multi-trillion-dollar AI capex intentions over the second half of this decade.

“Is it a bubble?”

This is a rare moment when valuation matters. Two groups drove the dot-com bubble:

  1. Pets.com and other empty “Dot-Com” businesses showing clicks and page views, but nothing else.
  2. Established tech giants that spent billions on the internet infrastructure.

We can see both today - cash-burning AI names on one side, and profitable tech leaders investing heavily on the other.

The difference is valuation. In 2000, multiples were extreme (Cisco (CSCO) above 200x earnings; Microsoft (MSFT) and Corning (GLW) above 65x).

Today’s AI leaders are richly valued, but generally nowhere near dot-com extremes (with a few stretched cases like AMD on the chart), and many sit closer to ~30x forward earnings.

So yes, bubble risk exists - mostly if hype and spending run too far - but there’s still room for growth into 2026. That’s why I stick with quality businesses that were strong before AI, using AI as an extra growth tailwind rather than the only thing holding up the dividend triangle.

If you’re reading this and thinking, “Okay, I get the theme... but what do I do with it?” you’re asking the right question.

Before We Get to Energy…

The big takeaway is that AI isn’t just a tech story anymore. It’s a macro force that’s driving a capex cycle across the economy - and it’s already reshaping what wins and what struggles.

But this is where things get interesting. When the economy starts spending at this scale, it creates bottlenecks. And the first bottleneck is obvious: energy.

In Part 2, I’ll walk through the “real-world” themes that AI is accelerating - power and grids, infrastructure and reshoring, the rise of private capital, and even the return of gold as a serious macro trade - without turning your portfolio into a trend-chasing machine.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams: Don't Paint Over This Excellent Opportunity
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

When was the last time you painted a room in your house?

A better question may be, do you have a room in your house that needs to be painted, and you just haven't painted it yet?

According to Better Homes and Gardens, the exterior of your house should be painted about once every 5 years. Possibly more, depending on sun damage or other weather impacts on your home. The inside of your house, however, likely gets more attention for painting, but actually needs it to be painted less often. They recommend waiting no more than 5-15 years to paint each interior room.

For many of us, we like to touch up rooms or paint rooms a different color more frequently than what's considered to be the "maximum" we should wait, because it can refresh or provide a facelift to a home, especially in the face of needing to sell it. To maximize value, a freshly painted home can sell for significantly more than a home with outdated paint by 2%-5% of the house's overall value.

I view paint similarly to how I view cleaning chemicals. People will always be painting some part of their house or some part of their property, just like they will always be cleaning some part of their home or property. While you may not consider paint to be an essential commodity in today's society, it quietly is a societal must that paint be prevalent and available, especially for those who enjoy refreshing their home. Today, I wanted to look at a way to unlock dividends and value from paint.

Strap on your painter's clothes and dive in!

A Dividend Aristocrat With Pricing Power

The general investment thesis for The Sherwin-Williams Company (SHW) is reflected by some simple facts.

The company is the largest player in the global coatings industry, operating over 5,400 stores throughout the U.S., Canada, the Caribbean, and Latin America. Why does this matter? Well, this provides the company with an unmatched "last-mile" distribution network. The sheer number of stores means that there's a high probability that professional contractors are just miles away from an SHW store. That immediate inventory availability and physical presence are a major competitive advantage for the company.

Another strength of SHW's business model is the fact that labor accounts for the vast majority of project costs for a professional contractor. This is why most of SHW's pro contractor customers are willing to tolerate steady price increases from the company. It's usually not worth switching to pinch a few pennies.

SHW put up decent results in Q3 2025. Its total net sales grew by 3.2% year-over-year to $6.36 billion during the quarter. That beat Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus for the quarter by $160 million. SHW's adjusted diluted EPS rose by 6.5% over the year-ago period to $3.59 in the quarter. This topped the consensus by $0.15 during the quarter.

The rate cut cycle playing out should be a growth driver for SHW. As mortgage rates eventually work their way down into the 5% range, demand for its products should eventually improve. This is because home sales should strengthen under such conditions. Its ongoing restructuring actions (i.e., a temporary pause of 401(k) matching contributions) can also help profit margins to gradually expand.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst adjusted diluted EPS consensus anticipates high single-digit to low double-digit percentage growth for 2026 through 2028. That would be off an anticipated 2025 base of $11.37.

SHW's balance sheet is investment-grade, too. The company sports a BBB S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

SHW's 0.9% dividend yield is modest, but its dividend growth rate has been solid. In the last five years, the company has grown its payout by 12.1% annually. This is enough for a Chowder number of 13, which I believe can be largely maintained moving forward. This is because SHW's payout ratio is set to be in the mid- to high-20% range for 2026. That gives it room to extend its 47-year dividend growth streak.

SHW doesn't stand out as an outright bargain. In my view, this fits more in the wonderful business at a fair price category. From the current $340 share price, SHW stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 27.3 (98% weight for 2026 and 2% weight for 2027). This is modestly less than the seven-year average P/E ratio of 28.7.

Moving forward, I think that the seven-year average P/E ratio is a rational, fair value estimate for SHW. The company's annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus of 10.1% is in line with the seven-year CAGR of 12.3%. This points to a company with intact growth potential trading at a 5% discount to fair value ($357 fair value per share estimate).

If SHW matches growth forecasts and returns to fair value, it could generate a more modest 6% total return in 2026. By the end of 2030, the stock could put up approximately 11% annual total returns.

Key risks to watch with SHW include a rapid rise in raw material costs and a sustained housing market downturn.

The most important raw material for the paint industry is arguably titanium dioxide. This comprises a meaningful portion of SHW's cost of goods sold. A pronounced and prolonged spike in spot prices for the raw material could pressure margins if it can't pass higher costs onto customers without pushback.

Another risk to SHW is the role that the housing market plays in its results. On the one hand, high interest rates are keeping existing homeowners in place. This drives demand for renovations via residential repaints. On the other hand, it pressures existing home sales.

The most recent data from Dodge Construction Network's Economics team showed a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.4 million privately owned housing starts in November. That was up 1.7% over the September estimate and 8.7% over the November 2024 estimate. This should lead to strong demand for SHW in the coming months on that front, with the caveat that any slowdown in housing starts could weigh on growth.

Conclusion

SHW is a wonderful company at a fair value. Looking forward, we expect economic conditions and the Trump administration's efforts to drive housing affordability to boost both top and bottom line earnings for SHW. This means SHW is an opportunity for reliable returns and growing dividends that shouldn't be painted over.

Dividend investing isn't just for retirees. Dividend investing is an essential form of investing that can benefit retirees and young investors alike. The longer you can hold a dividend-growing company, the greater the dividend will be compared to what you paid for it. You may spend 40 years or more working towards retirement, but you're also going to spend, hopefully, 30-plus years in retirement, meaning that you don't need to radically alter how you invest based on what age you are, especially when you're buying high-quality dividend growth companies that provide outsized returns.

Retirement can be a stressful time all by itself, entering a new paradigm of life where you're no longer punching the clock Monday through Friday for a paycheck from an employer. You don't need to add the stress of completely changing how you invest in the market to that equation. Dividend investing can provide you with a steady paycheck, a growing paycheck, and high levels of capital preservation and growth. This is the kind of portfolio that we encourage every single investor to heavily consider creating within Dividend Kings. Remove the chaos from your finances and embrace clarity.

data.title: Celestica Q4 Preview: Addressing Google Cloud Rumors (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica (CLS) is set to deliver its Q4 CY25 earnings results next Thursday, January 29, before markets open.

This Canadian AI networking company usually stays out of the spotlight, focused on delivering networking and connectivity solutions to its hyperscaler customers. The reality is that Celestica has been in absolute beast mode, with revenues expected to grow 27% this year and accelerate 31% next year. The company's hyperscaler customers appear to be satisfied with the company allocating an increasing amount of capex dollars towards Celestica's switches and server assembly solutions.

That is until this report that was published yesterday speculating a top hyperscaler client may be moving its server assembly orders elsewhere.

I expect Celestica to confidently address these rumors about its top hyperscaler client next week and believe the company is well positioned to benefit from another year of strong hyperscaler capex deployments.

I am upgrading Celestica's shares to a bullish view ahead of the Q4 ER next week.

Restore Investor Confidence

It has been a while since I covered Celestica on this platform. In June last year, I downgraded Celestica's shares to a neutral view after I told investors that I would be trimming a small portion of my shares. Celestica's shares had aggressively appreciated since last year's April bottom, and that pushed me to trim some of my shares at the time.

But Celestica's shares have surged 2x since my coverage last June, as its top hyperscaler customers continued to pour their respective capex dollars into Celestica's HPC switching and server assembly solutions. The company's performance has obviously superseded my neutral view that I issued last year, and that's purely because of the marvel in how well run this company is.

Here's a chart that I made to frame management's initial projections for the following year's revenue growth guide versus the actual revenue growth that Celestica ended up demonstrating. Note that CY25 & CY26 revenue growth rates are based on management's projections announced in the Q3 ER and 2025 Investor Day, respectively.

As can be seen above, there usually is a wide gap between what Celestica's management projects in the first guide for the following year versus the actual revenue growth that Celestica eventually ends up delivering. As the year progresses, management usually keeps raising their projections, which I have not captured in this chart, but the delta between the initial guide and actual revenue growth signals the strength in Celestica's networking business, which so far has shown no signs of stopping.

I'll frame the perspective in the paragraph above in a different way. Revenue growth has only accelerated every year, with the company well on track to deliver $12.2B in CY25 revenue, per management's projections, implying 27% growth, accelerating from CY24's revenue growth of 21%. That kind of revenue growth and that kind of revenue acceleration has resulted in Celestica winning meaningful market share in the data center switch market. This just shows how well positioned Celestica is in serving demand for its data center switch business, the strength of the company's relationships with its key hyperscaler customers, and the robustness of the company's products and solutions. That's also reflected in the company's product inventory levels, where the company is holding inventory for just 65 days-that's how quickly Celestica is turning its inventory into product revenue.

In the 2025 Investor Day, Celestica's management raised their CY26 projections well above expectations. CY26 revenue projections eventually landed at ~$16B, well above prior expectations of $13.6B. Operating margin guidance has also been beefed up by 40 bp to 7.8% from 7.4% in CY25.

Why does all this context framing matter for Celestica's investors?

Because of yesterday's report that speculated that Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Google Cloud, one of Celestica's largest hyperscaler customers, was looking at moving some of its server assembly business away from Celestica, possibly towards the Taiwanese ODM company Inventec. Firstly, I am apprehensive about placing trust in the report purely due to the timing of the report before Celestica's ER next week. Secondly, the report does not fully state whether it is an existing batch of purchase orders that are being moved over to Inventec or if it is a portion of Google Cloud's 2026-27 purchase orders for TPU-based server assembly that is being shared between Celestica, Inventec, or other vendors.

This isn't the first time this kind of speculation has surfaced. In Q2 last year, Celestica's management had to correct an analyst who speculated that the company may have "lost a little bit" of server market share last year. In that call, management reiterated that, in reality, the company was "gaining share with our [their] largest customers" and that the company would benefit from "significant momentum" as these server programs would begin ramping up from Q3 25. In the Q3 call, management reiterated the strength in the business, supported by a sustained ramp in program wins, which I believe gave Celestica's management enough conviction to not only raise revenue growth targets, implying 31% growth this year, but also expect strong growth in free cash to ~$500M.

Therefore, I fully expect management to reiterate their strength in the switching and server business while hashing out details around program wins regarding Google Cloud in the wake of this speculative report pertaining to possible server market share loss.

Outside of the clarifications that I expect management to provide regarding Google Cloud-related customer backlog, I am particularly interested in understanding the strength of their 1.6 Tb networking program solutions. The networking industry should see growth in 800G networking solutions mature while 1.6Tb solutions start to ramp up, representing the next phase of networking growth. Plus, Ethernet is enjoying the best phase of its revolutionary moment in the post-ChatGPT era, and scale-up networks are at a critical juncture of transitioning over towards Ethernet adoption in scale-up. These two developments represent robust target opportunities for Celestica, and I'm very curious about just how well positioned the company is.

Valuations Might Be Slightly Rich

Heading into earnings, investors might view Celestica's valuation multiples as marginal/moderately rich. The company's shares trade at 52x CY25 earnings or 35x CY26 earnings.

Focus on CY25's earnings that are expected to now grow 53% to $5.93 versus 24% at the start of last year. Part of the reason was because Celestica was able to revise up its revenue growth rates through CY25 as demand for its networking solutions. The other reason for superior earnings growth rates was due to strong margin expansion that led to significant earnings revisions leading to the 53% growth in EPS that Celestica is now expecting in CY25.

I believe this combination of strong demand and superior execution by management will lead to another strong year for Celestica, which currently trades at 35x CY26 earnings and is expected to grow ~40% to $8.2 per share. In my view, the current CY26 earnings multiple reflects appropriate entry points for investors once again after the company's valuation multiple looked foamy in Q4 last year.

Risks & What To Know Ahead Of Q4 ER

The big risk here is if Celestica's management actually ends up acknowledging the speculative nature of the report regarding Google Cloud's server assembly program. I believe this could be tied to Google attempting to significantly increase its own DC capacity. At a recent all-hands meeting, Google's execs revealed that they plan to "double AI serving capacity every 6 months." Google may be roping in additional vendors too to serve the search giant's aggressive timeline of adding DC capacity, in my view.

At a macro level, the larger risk is if AI demand starts to slow, which would push hyperscalers to push out their capex deployment timelines, impacting vendors like Celestica.

What I will be watching in next week's ER is if management raises its CY26 projections. Current projections are:

  • CY26 revenues of $16B, imputing to 31% top-line growth.
  • CY26 operating margins are expanding by 40 bp to 7.8%.
  • CY26 adj. EPS of $8.2 per share, imputing to ~40% EPS growth.
  • Free cash of ~$500M

Additionally, I would be looking forward to clarifications about Celestica's server program status with Google Cloud, whether they were able to win more programs or newer client programs.

One last note for investors: insider selling activity appears to be very positive for investors with minimal activity. This is another positive indicator for the strong times ahead.

Takeaway

I believe Celestica continues to enjoy robust AI tailwinds, with its key hyperscaler customer continuing to show faith in the company's networking and server assembly solutions.

Regardless of speculative reports, Celestica will continue to be a beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout race, and I am upgrading my view on Celestica to bullish for now.

data.title: Why I'm Betting The Majority Of My Portfolio On A Cyclical Boom
data.description:
Introduction

When I say that I run a somewhat unorthodox portfolio, I doubt it comes as a surprise to many readers, as most of you are fully aware that I run a very concentrated portfolio of just 16 individual companies, where the biggest five have a weighting of roughly 50%.

Here's what that portfolio looked like as of last weekend:

I'm not going to do a full breakdown of my positions, which is something I will do in the not-too-distant future. I'm bringing this up because slightly more than 60% of my portfolio is invested in stocks that tend to follow what I like to call the economic cycle. In other words, these are companies that tend to perform much better when investors get a signal of broadening and strengthening cyclical growth.

This includes my two biggest energy holdings, my investment in homebuilding-focused companies, all three of my transportation stocks, and my Canadian oil and gas producer.

Especially last year, I invested a lot of capital in cyclical value stocks. It wasn't a popular thesis, as cyclical growth was a mess. That's confirmed by indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which has been below the neutral 50 line for multiple years.

In December, the index took another hit, as we can see below.

This is also one of the reasons why the Mag-7 stocks did so well. They are mostly anti-cyclic, as manufacturing sentiment and housing demand have little impact on their business models. Moreover, when AI became mainstream, it was easy for the market to make the choice to invest in these stocks instead of struggling cyclical stocks.

That decision made sense.

However, as I am a contrarian when it comes to putting capital to work, I used the past year to accumulate cyclical value stocks. It's the reason I did not outperform the market in 2025. However, I believe it will also be the reason why I have a great shot at beating the market in 2026 and likely beyond.

I am so confident because, for the first time, my thesis is getting strong support from fundamental data. That's what this article is all about. I'll walk you through the numbers, explain why they confirm my thesis, and what this means going forward.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

To Me, This Looks Like A Serious Breakout

I believe that in order to fully understand what is going on here, we need to focus on (leading) indicators that actually matter. After all, there's so much data out there that it's easy to lose track. Personally, I look at leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which I just mentioned in the intro.

However, I also look into other numbers, including leading data from the Empire State (New York) and the Philadelphia Fed manufacturing surveys. These are reported ahead of the ISM index. And while they are more volatile and only cover a part of the U.S., when combined, they are a powerful leading indicator that I have been using for more than a decade.

In recent years, both have given off mixed signals. While both were weak (just like the ISM Index), they often gave false recovery signals, as we had many instances where one of them surged while the other declined. It basically told us that the cycle is bottoming, but not at all strengthening. Last year, that was the correct call.

Now, we're seeing more synchronized growth, as the Philly Fed General Activity Index surged to 12.6 points, which is its highest level since September. The Empire State General Business Conditions Index rose to 7.7 points after a dip in December.

Here's what the Empire State numbers look like:

This is the Philly Fed chart:

When we combine them, we see that the industrial economy is moving from contraction into expansion. If this persists, we can officially make the case that the economy is going from a "soft landing" scenario to a "no landing" scenario, which means the U.S. is likely to avoid a recession altogether.

This is important, as I have often made the case that we're in for higher inflation, but not a recession. My issue with the stagflation thesis that many had was that the "flation" part was right, but the "stag" part isn't confirmed. The opposite seems to be happening.

Needless to say, you could make the case that this is just another fake upswing signal. After all, we had many over the past 12-14 months. That would be a great comment and something we need to address to give my thesis a strong foundation.

I believe this upswing is for real, as we have a number of tailwinds hitting the "real" economy.

  • AI hyperscalers are "plowing" hundreds of billions into the economy this year by building data centers. This capital is expected to accelerate going forward (see below) and end up in construction, energy, and everything related to data centers, power generation, and the grid. I like to call this QE-like data center spending.
